title: Die weiße Schlange
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 17. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The seventeenth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 17), one of the great ‘grateful animals’ stories. A trusted servant secretly tastes the king’s covered dish of white snake and gains the power to understand the speech of animals; cleared of a theft when a duck yields up the queen’s ring, he rides out into the world and spares three fish, an ant-king and a brood of ravens, who in turn repay him — fetching a ring from the sea-bed, sorting ten sacks of millet, and bringing an apple from the tree of life — and so win him the proud princess. Their refrain, “wir wollen dirs gedenken und dirs vergelten,” sounds three times. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, mußte, wußte, gieng, Thür, Thäter, Thiere, Genuß, betheuerte, theilten, tödtete and ward.

sentences.t: Es ist nun schon lange her, da lebte ein König, dessen Weisheit im ganzen Lande berühmt war.
sentences.l: It is now already long ago, there lived a king whose wisdom was famous in the whole land.
sentences.i: It is long ago now that there lived a king whose wisdom was famed throughout the whole land.
sentences.n: “Es ist nun schon lange her” = ‘it is a long time ago now,’ a storyteller’s opening. “dessen” is the genitive relative ‘whose.’ “im ganzen Lande” keeps the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Nichts blieb ihm unbekannt, und es war als ob ihm Nachricht von den verborgensten Dingen durch die Luft zugetragen würde.
sentences.l: Nothing remained unknown to him, and it was as if news of the most hidden things were carried to him through the air.
sentences.i: Nothing remained unknown to him, and it was as though tidings of the most secret things were borne to him through the air.
sentences.n: “als ob ... würde” = ‘as if ... were’ — the subjunctive after als ob (‘as if’). “zugetragen würde” is a passive of zutragen, to carry to (dative ihm).

sentences.t: Er hatte aber eine seltsame Sitte.
sentences.l: But he had a strange custom.

sentences.t: Jeden Mittag, wenn von der Tafel alles abgetragen und niemand mehr zugegen war, mußte ein vertrauter Diener noch eine Schüssel bringen.
sentences.l: Every noon, when everything had been cleared from the table and no one was present any more, a trusted servant had to bring yet one more dish.
sentences.i: Every noon, when everything had been cleared away from the table and no one was present any longer, a trusted servant had to bring in one more dish.
sentences.n: “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste. “Tafel” is the (grand) dining table. “zugegen sein” = to be present, at hand.

sentences.t: Sie war aber zugedeckt, und der Diener wußte selbst nicht was darin lag, und kein Mensch wußte es, denn der König deckte sie nicht eher auf und aß nicht davon, bis er ganz allein war.
sentences.l: But it was covered over, and the servant himself did not know what lay in it, and no person knew it, for the king did not uncover it and did not eat of it until he was quite alone.
sentences.i: But it was covered, and the servant himself did not know what lay inside, and no one knew, for the king would not uncover it or eat of it until he was quite alone.
sentences.n: “Sie” refers to die Schüssel (the dish, fem.). “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste. “deckte ... auf” (aufdecken, to uncover) is separable; “nicht eher ... bis” = ‘not until.’

sentences.t: Das hatte schon lange Zeit gedauert, da überkam eines Tages den Diener, der die Schüssel wieder wegtrug, die Neugierde, daß er nicht widerstehen konnte, sondern die Schüssel in seine Kammer brachte.
sentences.l: That had already lasted a long time, when one day curiosity came over the servant, who was carrying the dish away again, so that he could not resist, but carried the dish into his chamber.
sentences.i: This had gone on for a long time, when one day, as the servant was carrying the dish away again, curiosity came over him so that he could not resist, but took the dish off to his room.
sentences.n: “überkam ... die Neugierde” — the curiosity ‘came over’ him (überkommen + accusative den Diener). “daß” is the 1857 dass. “nicht ... sondern” = ‘not ... but rather.’ “wegtrug” (wegtragen) is separable.

sentences.t: Als er die Thür sorgfältig verschlossen hatte, hob er den Deckel auf und da sah er daß eine weiße Schlange darin lag.
sentences.l: When he had carefully locked the door, he lifted the lid up, and there he saw that a white snake lay in it.
sentences.i: When he had carefully locked the door, he lifted the lid, and there he saw a white snake lying inside.
sentences.n: “Thür” carries the old -th- (modern Tür). “hob ... auf” (aufheben, to lift up) is separable. This white snake is the story’s magic: eating of it will let him understand the speech of animals.

sentences.t: Bei ihrem Anblick konnte er die Lust nicht zurückhalten, sie zu kosten; er schnitt ein Stückchen davon ab und steckte es in den Mund.
sentences.l: At the sight of it he could not hold back the desire to taste it; he cut a little piece off it and put it into his mouth.
sentences.i: At the sight of it he could not restrain his desire to taste it; he cut off a little piece and put it in his mouth.
sentences.n: “Bei ihrem Anblick” — ihrem refers to the Schlange (fem.). “zurückhalten” (to hold back) is separable. “schnitt ... ab” (abschneiden) is separable; “Stückchen” is the diminutive of Stück.

sentences.t: Kaum aber hatte es seine Zunge berührt, so hörte er vor seinem Fenster ein seltsames Gewisper von feinen Stimmen.
sentences.l: But scarcely had it touched his tongue, then he heard before his window a strange whispering of fine voices.
sentences.i: But scarcely had it touched his tongue when he heard, outside his window, a strange whispering of delicate voices.
sentences.n: “Kaum ... so” = ‘scarcely ... when’; the inversion (verb first) marks the ‘no sooner ... than’ frame. “es” refers to the little piece (das Stückchen, neuter).

sentences.t: Er gieng und horchte, da merkte er daß es die Sperlinge waren, die mit einander sprachen und sich allerlei erzählten, was sie im Felde und Walde gesehen hatten.
sentences.l: He went and listened; there he noticed that it was the sparrows, who were talking with one another and telling each other all sorts of things that they had seen in field and wood.
sentences.i: He went and listened, and noticed that it was the sparrows talking with one another, telling each other all kinds of things they had seen in field and forest.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging. “horchte” (horchen) = to listen intently. “im Felde und Walde” keep the old dative -e. Thanks to the snake he now understands the birds — the tale’s central gift.

sentences.t: Der Genuß der Schlange hatte ihm die Fähigkeit verliehen, die Sprache der Thiere zu verstehen.
sentences.l: The eating of the snake had bestowed on him the ability to understand the speech of the animals.
sentences.i: Eating the snake had granted him the power to understand the language of the animals.
sentences.n: “Genuß” (1857 spelling of Genuss) here means the partaking/eating, not merely ‘enjoyment.’ “Thiere” has the old -th- (modern Tiere). This is the gift that drives the whole tale — from here he understands ducks, ants and ravens.

sentences.t: Nun trug es sich zu, daß gerade an diesem Tage der Königin ihr schönster Ring fort kam und auf den vertrauten Diener, der überall Zugang hatte, der Verdacht fiel er habe ihn gestohlen.
sentences.l: Now it came about that on this very day the queen’s most beautiful ring went missing, and on the trusted servant, who had access everywhere, the suspicion fell that he had stolen it.
sentences.i: Now it came to pass that on that very day the queen’s finest ring went missing, and suspicion fell on the trusted servant, who had access everywhere, that he had stolen it.
sentences.n: “trug es sich zu” (sich zutragen) = ‘it came about,’ a solemn fairy-tale phrase. “der Königin ihr schönster Ring” is a colloquial possessive, ‘the queen’s finest ring.’ “fort kam” (fortkommen, to go missing) is separable. “er habe ... gestohlen” is subjunctive I of reported suspicion.

sentences.t: Der König ließ ihn vor sich kommen und drohte ihm unter heftigen Scheltworten wenn er bis morgen den Thäter nicht zu nennen wüßte, so sollte er dafür angesehen und gerichtet werden.
sentences.l: The king had him come before him and threatened him amid fierce words of scolding: if by tomorrow he did not know how to name the culprit, then he should be regarded as guilty of it and sentenced.
sentences.i: The king summoned him and, with harsh rebukes, threatened him: if by the next day he could not name the culprit, he would be held to blame for it and put to death.
sentences.n: “ließ ihn vor sich kommen” = had him brought before him (lassen + infinitive). “Thäter” has the old -th- (modern Täter, ‘culprit’). “wüßte” is subjunctive II of wissen. “angesehen” (ansehen) here = to regard (as guilty); “gerichtet” (richten) = judged, sentenced.

sentences.t: Es half nichts daß er seine Unschuld betheuerte, er ward mit keinem bessern Bescheid entlassen.
sentences.l: It helped nothing that he protested his innocence; he was dismissed with no better answer.
sentences.i: It availed nothing that he protested his innocence; he was sent away with no better answer.
sentences.n: “Es half nichts, daß ...” = ‘it was no use that ...’ “betheuerte” is the 1857 spelling of beteuerte (to protest, avow). “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden, here forming the passive ‘was dismissed.’

sentences.t: In seiner Unruhe und Angst gieng er hinab auf den Hof und bedachte wie er sich aus seiner Noth helfen könne.
sentences.l: In his disquiet and fear he went down into the courtyard and pondered how he might help himself out of his distress.
sentences.i: In his disquiet and dread he went down into the courtyard and pondered how he might get himself out of his trouble.
sentences.n: “gieng” again (1857 for ging). “Noth” carries the old -th- (modern Not, ‘need, distress’). “könne” is subjunctive I in the indirect question.

sentences.t: Da saßen die Enten an einem fließenden Wasser friedlich neben einander und ruhten, sie putzten sich mit ihren Schnäbeln glatt und hielten ein vertrauliches Gespräch.
sentences.l: There the ducks sat peacefully by a flowing water beside one another and rested; they preened themselves smooth with their beaks and held an intimate conversation.
sentences.i: There the ducks sat resting peacefully side by side by a running stream; they preened themselves smooth with their bills and held a cosy conversation.
sentences.n: “putzten sich ... glatt” = preened themselves smooth. Because of the snake he can follow the ducks’ talk — the tale’s second group of animals, and the one that clears him.

sentences.t: Der Diener blieb stehen und hörte ihnen zu.
sentences.l: The servant stopped and listened to them.
sentences.i: The servant stood still and listened to them.
sentences.n: “blieb stehen” = stopped, stood still (stehen bleiben). “hörte ... zu” (zuhören, to listen to) is separable and takes the dative ihnen.

sentences.t: Sie erzählten sich wo sie heute Morgen all herumgewackelt wären und was für gutes Futter sie gefunden hätten, da sagte eine verdrießlich „mir liegt etwas schwer im Magen, ich habe einen Ring, der unter der Königin Fenster lag, in der Hast mit hinunter geschluckt.“
sentences.l: They told each other where they had all waddled about this morning and what good fodder they had found, when one said peevishly, “something lies heavy in my stomach; in my haste I have swallowed down a ring that was lying under the queen’s window.”
sentences.i: They were telling each other where they had all waddled about that morning and what good food they had found, when one said sullenly, “Something lies heavy in my stomach; in my haste I gulped down a ring that was lying under the queen’s window.”
sentences.n: “wären ... hätten” are subjunctive of reported speech (the ducks’ own account). “herumgewackelt” (herumwackeln, to waddle about) is separable. “der Königin Fenster” = the queen’s window (bare genitive). “mit hinunter geschluckt” — hinunterschlucken, to swallow down.

sentences.t: Da packte sie der Diener gleich beim Kragen, trug sie in die Küche und sprach zum Koch „schlachte doch diese ab, sie ist wohl genährt.“
sentences.l: Then the servant at once seized it by the collar, carried it into the kitchen and said to the cook, “do slaughter this one, it is well nourished.”
sentences.i: At that the servant seized it by the scruff at once, carried it into the kitchen and said to the cook, “Do slaughter this one — she’s nicely fattened.”
sentences.n: “sie” is the duck (die Ente, fem.). “schlachte ... ab” — abschlachten, to slaughter (imperative schlachte, prefix ab to the end). “doch” is a coaxing particle. “wohl genährt” = well-fed, plump.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sagte der Koch, und wog sie in der Hand, „die hat keine Mühe gescheut sich zu mästen und schon lange darauf gewartet gebraten zu werden.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the cook, and weighed it in his hand, “this one has spared no effort to fatten itself and has long waited to be roasted.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” said the cook, weighing her in his hand, “this one has spared no pains to fatten herself and has long been waiting to be roasted.”
sentences.n: “wog” is the preterite of wiegen (to weigh). “keine Mühe gescheut” = spared no effort (Mühe scheuen, to shirk trouble). “gebraten zu werden” = to be roasted, a passive infinitive.

sentences.t: Er schnitt ihr den Hals ab, und als sie ausgenommen ward, fand sich der Ring der Königin in ihrem Magen.
sentences.l: He cut its throat off, and when it was gutted, the queen’s ring was found in its stomach.
sentences.i: He cut off her neck, and when she was drawn, the queen’s ring was found in her stomach.
sentences.n: “schnitt ihr den Hals ab” — abschneiden with dative ihr (‘cut off its neck’). “ausgenommen ward” — ausnehmen, to gut/draw (poultry); ward is the archaic passive. “fand sich” = was found (reflexive).

sentences.t: Der Diener konnte nun leicht vor dem Könige seine Unschuld beweisen, und da dieser sein Unrecht wieder gut machen wollte, erlaubte er ihm sich eine Gnade auszubitten und versprach ihm die größte Ehrenstelle, die er sich an seinem Hofe wünschte.
sentences.l: The servant could now easily prove his innocence before the king, and since the latter wanted to make good his wrong again, he allowed him to beg himself a favour and promised him the highest post of honour that he might wish for at his court.
sentences.i: The servant could now easily prove his innocence before the king; and since the king wished to make amends for his wrong, he allowed him to ask a favour, and promised him the highest post of honour he might wish for at his court.
sentences.n: “dem Könige” and “Hofe” keep the old dative -e. “dieser” (‘the latter’) points back to the king. “wieder gut machen” = to make amends. “sich eine Gnade ausbitten” (sich ausbitten) = to beg a favour for oneself.

sentences.t: Der Diener schlug alles aus und bat nur um ein Pferd und Reisegeld, denn er hatte Lust die Welt zu sehen und eine Weile darin herum zu ziehen.
sentences.l: The servant refused everything and asked only for a horse and travelling money, for he had a desire to see the world and to roam about in it for a while.
sentences.i: The servant declined it all and asked only for a horse and travelling money, for he had a mind to see the world and to roam about in it for a while.
sentences.n: “schlug ... aus” (ausschlagen, to refuse, decline) is separable. “bat um” (bitten um) = to ask for. “Lust haben” = to have a mind/desire to. “herum ziehen” (herumziehen, to roam) is separable.

sentences.t: Als seine Bitte erfüllt war, machte er sich auf den Weg und kam eines Tags an einem Teich vorbei, wo er drei Fische bemerkte, die sich im Rohr gefangen hatten und nach Wasser schnappten.
sentences.l: When his request had been fulfilled, he set out on his way and one day came past a pond, where he noticed three fish that had caught themselves in the reeds and were gasping for water.
sentences.i: When his request had been granted, he set out on his way, and one day came past a pond where he saw three fish that had got caught in the reeds and were gasping for water.
sentences.n: “machte sich auf den Weg” = set out. “kam ... vorbei” (vorbeikommen, to come past) is separable. “nach Wasser schnappten” — schnappen nach, to snap/gasp for. These fish are the first of the three grateful animals.

sentences.t: Obgleich man sagt, die Fische wären stumm, so vernahm er doch ihre Klage daß sie so elend umkommen müßten.
sentences.l: Although one says the fish are mute, he nevertheless heard their lament that they must perish so miserably.
sentences.i: Although people say fish are dumb, he nonetheless heard their lament that they must perish so wretchedly.
sentences.n: “Obgleich” = although (concessive conjunction). “wären ... müßten” are subjunctive of reported speech (‘are said to be...’). “vernahm” (vernehmen) is elevated for ‘heard, perceived.’ “umkommen” = to perish.

sentences.t: Weil er ein mitleidiges Herz hatte, so stieg er vom Pferde ab und setzte die drei Gefangenen wieder ins Wasser.
sentences.l: Because he had a compassionate heart, he climbed down from the horse and put the three captives back into the water.
sentences.i: Because he had a compassionate heart, he got down from his horse and set the three captives back into the water.
sentences.n: “Weil ... so” — the ‘so’ resumes the main clause after the causal weil-clause. “stieg ... ab” (absteigen, to dismount) is separable; “Pferde” keeps the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Sie zappelten vor Freude, streckten die Köpfe heraus und riefen ihm zu „wir wollen dirs gedenken und dirs vergelten daß du uns errettet hast.“
sentences.l: They wriggled for joy, stretched their heads out and called to him, “we will remember it to you and repay it to you, that you have rescued us.”
sentences.i: They wriggled for joy, stretched out their heads and called to him, “We will not forget it, and will repay you for having rescued us.”
sentences.n: “riefen ... zu” (zurufen, to call to) is separable + dative. “dirs” = dir + es (‘it to you’). “wir wollen dirs gedenken und dirs vergelten” is the grateful animals’ set refrain — you will hear it three times, once from each helper. “errettet” (erretten) = rescued, delivered.

sentences.t: Er ritt weiter, und nach einem Weilchen kam es ihm vor als hörte er zu seinen Füßen in dem Sand eine Stimme.
sentences.l: He rode on, and after a little while it seemed to him as if he heard a voice at his feet in the sand.
sentences.i: He rode on, and after a little while it seemed to him as though he heard a voice at his feet in the sand.
sentences.n: “kam es ihm vor” (vorkommen, to seem) is separable + dative. “als hörte er” — subjunctive II after als (‘as if he heard’). “Weilchen” is the diminutive of Weile (a little while).

sentences.t: Er horchte und vernahm wie ein Ameisenkönig klagte „wenn uns nur die Menschen mit den ungeschickten Thieren vom Leib blieben! da tritt mir das dumme Pferd mit seinen schweren Hufen meine Leute ohne Barmherzigkeit nieder!“
sentences.l: He hearkened and heard how an ant-king lamented, “if only people would keep off our bodies with their clumsy beasts! there the stupid horse treads my people down with its heavy hooves without mercy!”
sentences.i: He listened and heard an ant-king lamenting, “If only people would keep off us with their clumsy beasts! Look how the stupid horse tramples my people down with its heavy hooves without mercy!”
sentences.n: “vom Leib bleiben” = to keep off, leave alone (dative uns). “blieben” is subjunctive II of the wish. “tritt ... nieder” (niedertreten, to trample down) is separable; “mir” is a dative of interest (‘on me’). “Thieren” has the old -th-. The ant-king is the second grateful helper.

sentences.t: Er lenkte auf einen Seitenweg ein und der Ameisenkönig rief ihm zu „wir wollen dirs gedenken und dirs vergelten.“
sentences.l: He turned onto a side-path, and the ant-king called to him, “we will remember it to you and repay it to you.”
sentences.i: He turned aside onto a byway, and the ant-king called to him, “We will not forget it, and will repay you.”
sentences.n: “lenkte ... ein” (einlenken, to turn onto) is separable. The refrain returns — the same words the fish used; the ravens will speak it too.

sentences.t: Der Weg führte ihn in einen Wald und da sah er einen Rabenvater und eine Rabenmutter, die standen bei ihrem Nest und warfen ihre Jungen heraus.
sentences.l: The way led him into a wood, and there he saw a raven-father and a raven-mother, who stood by their nest and were throwing their young out.
sentences.i: The road led him into a forest, and there he saw a father raven and a mother raven standing by their nest, throwing their young out.
sentences.n: “Rabenvater / Rabenmutter” — literally ‘raven-father / raven-mother,’ the parent ravens. “warfen ... heraus” (herauswerfen, to throw out) is separable. “Jungen” = young (of animals).

sentences.t: „Fort mit euch, ihr Galgenschwengel,“ riefen sie, „wir können euch nicht mehr satt machen, ihr seid groß genug, und könnt euch selbst ernähren.“
sentences.l: “Away with you, you gallows-birds,” they cried, “we can no longer make you full, you are big enough and can feed yourselves.”
sentences.i: “Off with you, you good-for-nothings!” they cried. “We can’t fill your bellies any longer; you’re big enough, and can feed yourselves.”
sentences.n: “Fort mit euch” = ‘away with you.’ “Galgenschwengel” (lit. ‘gallows-swing’) is a term of abuse, ‘gallows-bird, rascal.’ “satt machen” = to fill up, satisfy. “sich ernähren” = to feed oneself.

sentences.t: Die armen Jungen lagen auf der Erde, flatterten und schlugen mit ihren Fittichen und schrien „wir hilflosen Kinder, wir sollen uns selbst ernähren und können noch nicht fliegen! was bleibt uns übrig als hier Hungers zu sterben!“
sentences.l: The poor young lay on the ground, fluttered and beat with their wings and screamed, “we helpless children, we are to feed ourselves and cannot fly yet! what remains to us but to die of hunger here!”
sentences.i: The poor young lay on the ground, fluttering and beating their wings, and cried, “We helpless children — we are to feed ourselves and cannot yet fly! What is left for us but to die of hunger here!”
sentences.n: “Fittichen” is the dative plural of Fittich, an elevated word for ‘wing.’ “was bleibt uns übrig als” = ‘what is left for us but.’ “Hungers sterben” = to die of hunger (Hungers is an old genitive of cause).

sentences.t: Da stieg der gute Jüngling ab, tödtete das Pferd mit seinem Degen und überließ es den jungen Raben zum Futter.
sentences.l: Then the good youth dismounted, killed the horse with his sword and left it to the young ravens for fodder.
sentences.i: Then the good youth dismounted, killed the horse with his sword, and gave it over to the young ravens for food.
sentences.n: “stieg ... ab” (absteigen) is separable. “tödtete” is the 1857 spelling of tötete (töten, to kill). “überließ” (überlassen) = to leave/give over (dative den Raben). His own horse is the price of saving the ravens — the third grateful helper.

sentences.t: Die kamen herbeigehüpft, sättigten sich und riefen „wir wollen dirs gedenken und dirs vergelten.“
sentences.l: They came hopping up, sated themselves and cried, “we will remember it to you and repay it to you.”
sentences.i: They came hopping up, ate their fill, and cried, “We will not forget it, and will repay you.”
sentences.n: “Die” (‘they’) resumes the young ravens. “kamen herbeigehüpft” — herbeihüpfen, to come hopping up. “sättigten sich” (sich sättigen) = ate their fill. The refrain a third and final time — all three debts will be paid on the quest ahead.

sentences.t: Er mußte jetzt seine eigenen Beine gebrauchen, und als er lange Wege gegangen war, kam er in eine große Stadt.
sentences.l: He now had to use his own legs, and when he had gone long ways, he came into a great city.
sentences.i: Now he had to use his own legs, and when he had walked long roads, he came to a great city.
sentences.n: “mußte” is the 1857 spelling of musste. Having killed his horse for the ravens, he must now go on foot.

sentences.t: Da war großer Lärm und Gedränge in den Straßen, und kam einer zu Pferde und machte bekannt, „die Königstochter suche einen Gemahl, wer sich aber um sie bewerben wolle, der müsse eine schwere Aufgabe vollbringen, und könne er es nicht glücklich ausführen, so habe er sein Leben verwirkt.“
sentences.l: There was great noise and throng in the streets, and one came on horseback and made known, “the king’s daughter is seeking a husband; but whoever will woo her must accomplish a hard task, and if he cannot carry it out happily, then he has forfeited his life.”
sentences.i: There was a great din and crush in the streets, and a man came on horseback and proclaimed, “The king’s daughter is seeking a husband; but whoever would woo her must accomplish a hard task, and if he cannot bring it off, he has forfeited his life.”
sentences.n: “machte bekannt” (bekannt machen, to announce) is separable. The heralded speech runs in subjunctive I: “suche ... wolle ... müsse ... könne ... habe.” “sich um sie bewerben” = to sue for her hand. “sein Leben verwirkt” = has forfeited his life.

sentences.t: Viele hatten es schon versucht, aber vergeblich ihr Leben daran gesetzt.
sentences.l: Many had already attempted it, but staked their life on it in vain.
sentences.i: Many had already tried, but had staked their lives on it in vain.
sentences.n: “ihr Leben daran setzen” = to stake one’s life on it. “vergeblich” = in vain.

sentences.t: Der Jüngling, als er die Königstochter sah, ward er von ihrer großen Schönheit so verblendet, daß er alle Gefahr vergaß, vor den König trat und sich als Freier meldete.
sentences.l: The youth, when he saw the king’s daughter, was so dazzled by her great beauty that he forgot all danger, stepped before the king and presented himself as a suitor.
sentences.i: The youth, when he saw the king’s daughter, was so dazzled by her great beauty that he forgot all danger, stepped before the king, and offered himself as a suitor.
sentences.n: “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (here passive ‘was dazzled’); note the resumptive “er” after the fronted subject. “vergaß” is the preterite of vergessen. “sich melden als” = to present oneself as.

sentences.t: Alsbald ward er hinaus ans Meer geführt und vor seinen Augen ein goldener Ring hinein geworfen.
sentences.l: At once he was led out to the sea, and before his eyes a golden ring was thrown in.
sentences.n: “Alsbald” is elevated/archaic for ‘at once.’ “ward ... geführt” and “[ward] ... geworfen” are passives with the archaic ward. “hinaus ... geführt / hinein geworfen” — the hin- prefixes mark motion away and in.

sentences.t: Dann hieß ihn der König diesen Ring aus dem Meeresgrund wieder hervorzuholen, und fügte hinzu „wenn du ohne ihn wieder in die Höhe kommst, so wirst du immer aufs neue hinab gestürzt, bis du in den Wellen umkommst.“
sentences.l: Then the king bade him fetch this ring up again out of the sea-bottom, and added, “if you come up to the surface again without it, then you will be thrown down ever anew, until you perish in the waves.”
sentences.i: Then the king bade him bring this ring up again from the bottom of the sea, and added, “If you come up again without it, you shall be cast down again and again, until you perish in the waves.”
sentences.n: “hieß ihn ... hervorzuholen” — heißen + infinitive, ‘bade him fetch out.’ “fügte hinzu” (hinzufügen, to add) is separable. “aufs neue” = anew; “hinab gestürzt” (hinabstürzen) = cast down. “umkommst” = you perish.

sentences.t: Alle bedauerten den schönen Jüngling und ließen ihn dann einsam am Meere zurück.
sentences.l: All pitied the handsome youth and then left him behind alone by the sea.
sentences.i: Everyone pitied the handsome youth, and then left him behind alone by the sea.
sentences.n: “bedauerten” (bedauern) = pitied. “ließen ... zurück” (zurücklassen, to leave behind) is separable. “Meere” keeps the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Er stand am Ufer und überlegte was er wohl thun sollte, da sah er auf einmal drei Fische daher schwimmen, und es waren keine anderen, als jene, welchen er das Leben gerettet hatte.
sentences.l: He stood on the shore and considered what he should do, when all at once he saw three fish come swimming along, and they were none other than those whose life he had saved.
sentences.i: He stood on the shore and pondered what he ought to do, when all at once he saw three fish come swimming up — and they were none other than those whose lives he had saved.
sentences.n: “überlegte” = considered. “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’). “daher schwimmen” = to come swimming along. “welchen ... das Leben gerettet” — welchen is the dative relative. The first debt is repaid.

sentences.t: Der mittelste hielt eine Muschel im Munde, die er an den Strand zu den Füßen des Jünglings hinlegte, und als dieser sie aufhob und öffnete, so lag der Goldring darin.
sentences.l: The middle one held a shell in its mouth, which it laid on the shore at the feet of the youth, and when he lifted it up and opened it, the gold ring lay in it.
sentences.i: The middle one held a shell in its mouth, which it laid on the shore at the youth’s feet; and when he picked it up and opened it, there lay the gold ring inside.
sentences.n: “der mittelste” = the middlemost (superlative). “Munde” keeps the old dative -e. “hinlegte” (hinlegen, to lay down) and “aufhob” (aufheben, to pick up) are separable. “dieser” (‘the latter’) refers to the youth.

sentences.t: Voll Freude brachte er ihn dem Könige und erwartete daß er ihm den verheißenen Lohn gewähren würde.
sentences.l: Full of joy he brought it to the king and expected that he would grant him the promised reward.
sentences.i: Full of joy he brought it to the king and expected that the king would grant him the promised reward.
sentences.n: “dem Könige” keeps the old dative -e. “verheißenen” = promised (verheißen, elevated ‘to promise’). “gewähren würde” is subjunctive II of expectation.

sentences.t: Die stolze Königstochter aber, als sie vernahm, daß er ihr nicht ebenbürtig war, verschmähte ihn und verlangte er sollte zuvor eine zweite Aufgabe lösen.
sentences.l: But the proud king’s daughter, when she heard that he was not her equal in birth, spurned him and demanded that he should first solve a second task.
sentences.i: But the proud king’s daughter, when she learned that he was not her equal in birth, spurned him and demanded that he first solve a second task.
sentences.n: “ebenbürtig” = of equal birth/rank (dative ihr, ‘equal to her’). “verschmähte” (verschmähen) = spurned. “sollte ... lösen” — a reported command with sollen. “zuvor” = beforehand, first.

sentences.t: Sie gieng hinab in den Garten und streute selbst zehn Säcke voll Hirsen ins Gras.
sentences.l: She went down into the garden and herself strewed ten sacks full of millet into the grass.
sentences.i: She went down into the garden and herself scattered ten sacks of millet into the grass.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging. “streute” (streuen) = strewed, scattered. “Hirsen” = millet. This is the impossible sorting-task the ants will solve.

sentences.t: „Die muß er Morgen, eh die Sonne hervor kommt, aufgelesen haben,“ sprach sie, „und darf kein Körnchen fehlen.“
sentences.l: “He must have gathered them up by tomorrow, ere the sun comes forth,” she spoke, “and not a little grain may be missing.”
sentences.i: “He must have gathered it all up by tomorrow, before the sun comes up,” she said, “and not a single grain may be missing.”
sentences.n: “Die” refers to the millet grains. “eh” is a shortened ehe (‘before’). “hervor kommt” (hervorkommen, to come forth) is separable. “aufgelesen haben” — auflesen, to gather up. “Körnchen” is the diminutive of Korn (little grain).

sentences.t: Der Jüngling setzte sich in den Garten und dachte nach wie es möglich wäre, die Aufgabe zu lösen, aber er konnte nichts ersinnen, saß da ganz traurig und erwartete bei Anbruch des Morgens zum Tode geführt zu werden.
sentences.l: The youth sat down in the garden and thought over how it might be possible to solve the task, but he could think nothing up, sat there quite sad and expected to be led to death at the break of morning.
sentences.i: The youth sat down in the garden and pondered how it might be possible to solve the task, but he could devise nothing; he sat there quite sorrowful and expected to be led to his death at daybreak.
sentences.n: “dachte nach” (nachdenken, to reflect) is separable. “wäre” is subjunctive II. “ersinnen” = to devise, think up. “bei Anbruch des Morgens” = at daybreak. “zum Tode geführt zu werden” is a passive infinitive; “Tode” keeps the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Als aber die ersten Sonnenstrahlen in den Garten fielen, so sah er die zehn Säcke alle wohl gefüllt neben einander stehen, und kein Körnchen fehlte darin.
sentences.l: But when the first rays of sun fell into the garden, he saw the ten sacks all standing well filled beside one another, and not a little grain was missing in them.
sentences.i: But when the first rays of sunlight fell into the garden, he saw the ten sacks standing side by side, all well filled, and not a grain was missing from them.
sentences.n: “Als ... so” — the ‘so’ resumes the main clause after the Als-clause. “wohl gefüllt” = well filled. “fehlte” (fehlen) = was missing.

sentences.t: Der Ameisenkönig war mit seinen tausend und tausend Ameisen in der Nacht angekommen, und die dankbaren Thiere hatten den Hirsen mit großer Emsigkeit gelesen und in die Säcke gesammelt.
sentences.l: The ant-king had arrived in the night with his thousand and thousand ants, and the grateful animals had gathered the millet with great diligence and collected it into the sacks.
sentences.i: The ant-king had arrived in the night with his thousands upon thousands of ants, and the grateful creatures had gathered the millet with great diligence and collected it into the sacks.
sentences.n: “angekommen” (ankommen, to arrive) is separable. “dankbaren Thiere” — the grateful animals; Thiere has the old -th-. “gelesen” — here lesen means ‘to gather, glean’ (its older sense), not ‘to read.’ The second debt is repaid.

sentences.t: Die Königstochter kam selbst in den Garten herab und sah mit Verwunderung daß der Jüngling vollbracht hatte was ihm aufgegeben war.
sentences.l: The king’s daughter came down into the garden herself and saw with astonishment that the youth had accomplished what had been set for him.
sentences.i: The king’s daughter came down into the garden herself and saw with astonishment that the youth had accomplished what had been set him.
sentences.n: “kam ... herab” (herabkommen, to come down) is separable. “vollbracht” (vollbringen) = accomplished. “aufgegeben” (aufgeben, here ‘to set, assign’) is separable; the passive ‘was set for him.’

sentences.t: Aber sie konnte ihr stolzes Herz noch nicht bezwingen und sprach „hat er auch die beiden Aufgaben gelöst, so soll er doch nicht eher mein Gemahl werden, bis er mir einen Apfel vom Baume des Lebens gebracht hat.“
sentences.l: But she could not yet subdue her proud heart and spoke, “even though he has solved the two tasks, he shall still not become my husband until he has brought me an apple from the tree of life.”
sentences.i: But she could not yet subdue her proud heart, and said, “Even if he has solved the two tasks, still he shall not become my husband until he has brought me an apple from the tree of life.”
sentences.n: “bezwingen” = to subdue, master. “hat er auch ... gelöst, so ...” — the fronted verb with auch means ‘even though he has solved’ (a concessive). “nicht eher ... bis” = ‘not until.’ “Baume” keeps the old dative -e; “des Lebens” is genitive.

sentences.t: Der Jüngling wußte nicht wo der Baum des Lebens stand, er machte sich auf und wollte immer zu gehen, so lange ihn seine Beine trügen, aber er hatte keine Hoffnung ihn zu finden.
sentences.l: The youth did not know where the tree of life stood; he set out and would keep going as long as his legs might carry him, but he had no hope of finding it.
sentences.i: The youth did not know where the tree of life stood; he set out, meaning to walk straight on as long as his legs would carry him, but he had no hope of finding it.
sentences.n: “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste. “machte sich auf” = set out. “immer zu gehen” — immerzu, ‘straight on, continually.’ “trügen” is subjunctive II of tragen (‘would carry’).

sentences.t: Als er schon durch drei Königreiche gewandert war und Abends in einen Wald kam, setzte er sich unter einen Baum und wollte schlafen: da hörte er in den Ästen ein Geräusch und ein goldner Apfel fiel in seine Hand.
sentences.l: When he had already wandered through three kingdoms and came in the evening into a wood, he sat down under a tree and wanted to sleep: there he heard a noise in the branches, and a golden apple fell into his hand.
sentences.i: When he had already wandered through three kingdoms and came at evening into a forest, he sat down under a tree and meant to sleep; then he heard a noise in the branches, and a golden apple fell into his hand.
sentences.n: “gewandert war” (wandern) = had wandered. “Ästen” is the dative plural of Ast (branch). “Geräusch” = noise, sound. “goldner” is a syncopated form of goldener.

sentences.t: Zugleich flogen drei Raben zu ihm herab, setzten sich auf seine Knie und sagten „wir sind die drei jungen Raben, die du vom Hungertod errettet hast; als wir groß geworden waren und hörten daß du den goldenen Apfel suchtest, so sind wir über das Meer geflogen bis ans Ende der Welt, wo der Baum des Lebens steht, und haben dir den Apfel geholt.“
sentences.l: At the same time three ravens flew down to him, sat down on his knees and said, “we are the three young ravens whom you rescued from death by hunger; when we had grown big and heard that you were seeking the golden apple, then we flew over the sea to the end of the world, where the tree of life stands, and have fetched you the apple.”
sentences.i: At the same time three ravens flew down to him, settled on his knees, and said, “We are the three young ravens you saved from starvation; when we had grown big and heard that you were seeking the golden apple, we flew over the sea to the end of the world, where the tree of life stands, and have fetched you the apple.”
sentences.n: “flogen ... herab” (herabfliegen) is separable. “Hungertod” = death by starvation. “suchtest” is the 2nd sg. preterite of suchen. The third and last debt is repaid — the ravens complete the quest for him.

sentences.t: Voll Freude machte sich der Jüngling auf den Heimweg und brachte der schönen Königstochter den goldenen Apfel, der nun keine Ausrede mehr übrig blieb.
sentences.l: Full of joy the youth set out on the homeward way and brought the beautiful king’s daughter the golden apple, to whom now no excuse remained left over.
sentences.i: Full of joy the youth set out for home and brought the beautiful king’s daughter the golden apple — and now no excuse was left to her.
sentences.n: “machte sich auf den Heimweg” = set out for home. “der ... keine Ausrede mehr übrig blieb” — der is a dative relative (‘to whom’), referring to the princess: no excuse remained to her. “übrig blieb” (übrigbleiben, to remain) is separable.

sentences.t: Sie theilten den Apfel des Lebens und aßen ihn zusammen: da ward ihr Herz mit Liebe zu ihm erfüllt, und sie erreichten in ungestörtem Glück ein hohes Alter.
sentences.l: They divided the apple of life and ate it together: then her heart was filled with love for him, and they reached a great age in undisturbed happiness.
sentences.i: They shared the apple of life and ate it together; then her heart was filled with love for him, and they reached a ripe old age in undisturbed happiness.
sentences.n: “theilten” is the 1857 spelling of teilten (teilen, to share/divide). “ward ... erfüllt” — the archaic passive again. “in ungestörtem Glück ein hohes Alter erreichen” = to reach a great age in undisturbed happiness, a closing formula.
